original_reasoning: The Arabic gloss centers on testing and finding out by palpation, while εὐέλεγκτος means easy to test, prove, or refute; the core of examinability survives.
Arabic الشجر and Greek olive/olive-land retain a direct tree/wooded referent with moderate phonetic drift.
Shortness maps plausibly to little heat through the core idea of small degree.
Rank, upright arrangement, and ordered degree align plausibly with a standardized measure.
Arabic centers on firmness, settledness, and stable positioning; Greek Larisa is often a citadel/fortified height, a place of firm standing.
Greek names a pale/silvery fish, and Arabic is 'whiteness of eggs'; both converge on a white/light color notion.
Arabic drive away or expel and Greek pass through share a motion-through or motion-away core.
Arabic evokes short dunes and interior hollows, while Greek is 'contain/comprise'; both converge on enclosure/interiority.
